summary: This notebook demonstrates an inference pipeline for the WSDM CUP LMSYS Chatbot Arena competition. It loads pre-trained Gemma2-9B and Llama3-8B models, formats chat prompts using a custom processor, and performs GPU inference with manual pipeline parallelism across two devices. The author shows how to handle variable-length sequences, compute prediction probabilities, and generate a final submission CSV. Readers learn how to adapt LLMs for pairwise preference tasks and optimize inference memory usage without relying on heavy orchestration frameworks.
competition_flow: Loads test data, formats prompts for Gemma2-9B and Llama3-8B, runs inference with manual pipeline parallelism across two GPUs, computes prediction probabilities, and exports a winner submission CSV.
data_reading: pandas.read_parquet on test.parquet (or train.parquet for local testing)
Dummy labels injected for local validation
data_processing: Custom ProcessorPAB constructs conversation templates with system/user roles and handles null values
ShardedMaxTokensCollator and VarlenCollator manage variable-length batching
Response swapping prepared for test-time augmentation
ensembling: Commented-out code shows an attempt to average predictions from Gemma2 and Llama3 with weights [2, 1] and swap model B predictions, but the final submission uses only the Llama3-8B probabilities.
insights: Manual pipeline parallelism across two GPUs can be implemented by splitting layer forward passes and transferring hidden states between devices.
Variable-length batching with token-sharded collators effectively manages VRAM during inference.
Swapping response order can help verify prediction consistency or act as a simple test-time augmentation.
